title: The Prose Life of Alexander
summary: The repository artifact is a pinned 1997 University of Michigan encoding of J. S. Westlake's 1913 EETS reading text. Westlake transcribed the unique, acephalous and internally defective Middle English witness in Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, ff. 1r–49r, and supplied the missing opening and two internal lacunae in Modern English from an unidentified Latin text. The medieval work is an anonymous translation-adaptation of the variable Latin Historia de preliis J3 recension, but no physical Latin exemplar has been identified. Later Latin incunabula and modern editions are therefore modeled only at their evidenced consultation or comparison scope, never as the medieval translator's exemplar.
review_notes: The cataloged intellectual subject is one anonymous Middle English prose romance, distinct from the sole manuscript copy, Westlake's composite reading text, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

Middle English is the work's primary language. Modern English occurs only in Westlake's representation-level supplies, while Latin occurs in the source-tradition relationship, three embedded verse couplets, and the explicit.

The eight DIV1 containers are edition or encoding sections, not asserted intrinsic chapters. The work remains prose for discovery because all eight L elements form one brief embedded prophecy cluster at printed pp. 94–95 / manuscript f. 40r.
The witness is acephalous and has at least one leaf missing after f. 18v and at least one after f. 19v. Westlake's opening and internal supplies are not represented as surviving Middle English text.

The 1489 and 1494 Strasbourg prints postdate the c. 1430–1450 witness. Westlake explicitly names both only in a selected note on printed p. 6; neither is asserted as his general supply text or as the medieval translator's exemplar.

No complete public digital facsimile of Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 was verified on 2026-07-15. The one public catalog image is part of f. 80r, outside the PLAlex component, while the complete Scolar photographic facsimile is cataloged for onsite consultation at Wellcome Collection.

Current U-M discovery metadata and the current Compendium XML label the item public domain / No Copyright–United States, while the pinned XML embeds a narrower personal, research, and teaching notice. Their relationship and the applicability of the CME repository's software-worded Unlicense remain unresolved.

The current Compendium XML and pinned artifact preserve identical Unicode-alphanumeric-normalized BODY text when the current file's two CORR alternatives are excluded in favor of its SIC readings. Their wrappers and element vocabulary differ, so they are modeled as versions rather than byte-identical files.

All eleven cached public source files were checked by checksum and byte count. The three PDFs passed qpdf integrity checks; both ZIP files passed archive tests; all three cached XML files are well formed; the Heidelberg bundle contains its canonical IIIF manifest, inventory, and all 74 listed image files.

entities.label: General Corpus pinned source XML for PLAlex
entities.description: The exact XML artifact consumed from the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME source at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
notes: The file is 282,762 bytes.
Its profile description's sole LANGUAGE value, Latin, is contradicted by the predominantly Middle English body.
Its BODY preserves Westlake's reading text but omits the printed prefatory note and most apparatus.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item PLAlex
alternate_labels: The Prose Life of Alexander from the Thornton MS.
entities.description: A 1997 electronic encoding of Westlake's EETS reading text, produced by the University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative from ScanWorx OCR and subsequent markup, proofreading, and review.
notes: The XML header credits ScanWorx OCR, Jason P. Williams for markup, Sean Pollack for proofreading, and Jason Chu for review.
The encoding represents the Modern English supplies as ADD elements and retains six NOTE1 elements, but it does not encode the edition's prefatory note or most marginal and footnote apparatus.

entities.label: Current Middle English Compendium PLAlex XML, last modified 9 May 2026
entities.description: The current ETS serialization distributed from medictionary.info. Its BODY preserves the pinned artifact's normalized text when two added CORR alternatives are excluded in favor of their SIC readings, while its wrapper, element vocabulary, correction encoding, and availability statement differ.

date_statements.label: HTTP resource last modified
date_statements.notes: The date is the provider's Last-Modified value, not a named release identifier.

notes: The research-local 2026-05 suffix distinguishes the accessed snapshot and is not provider-assigned.
This current serialization is not conflated with the separate U-M HTML interface or pinned DLPSTEXTCLASS artifact; the exact derivation direction between the two XML serializations is unknown.
It retains the legacy header's sole Latin language declaration even though the reading text is predominantly Middle English with editorial Modern English supplies.

entities.label: J. S. Westlake's The Prose Life of Alexander from the Thornton MS.
entities.description: The EETS reading text on printed pp. 1–115. It combines Westlake's transcription of the surviving Middle English witness with his Modern English translations from an unidentified Latin text at the lost opening and two internal lacunae.

identifiers.scheme: Early English Text Society Original Series

bibliographic.citation: The Prose Life of Alexander from the Thornton MS., ed. J. S. Westlake, Early English Text Society, Original Series 143 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1913, for 1911).
bibliographic.title: The Prose Life of Alexander from the Thornton MS.
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 143
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society

notes: The prefatory note says that illness prevented Westlake from revising and publishing the text himself; E. M. Thompson collated it against the manuscript and John Munro read the proofs.
The promised introduction, notes, and glossary were reserved for a future volume that was not published.
Westlake's Latin translations supply missing narrative and are editorial English, not reconstructed Middle English.

entities.label: Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, complete Lincoln Thornton Manuscript
entities.description: The complete mid-fifteenth-century paper miscellany written by Robert Thornton. Its first component is the unique surviving copy of the Middle English Prose Life of Alexander at ff. 1r–49r.

physical_description.extent: 322 leaves in current numbering; original minimum approximately 340 leaves
notes: The complete codex is attributed to the single scribe Robert Thornton.
The catalog says it was written by and for Thornton of East Newton, North Yorkshire.
This whole-codex entity allows non-PLAlex images and the complete Scolar facsimile to remain distinct from the ff. 1r–49r work component.

entities.label: Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, Prose Life of Alexander component
entities.description: Robert Thornton's sole surviving copy of the Middle English Prose Life of Alexander, the first item in the Lincoln Thornton Manuscript. The component begins imperfectly and has internal losses after ff. 18v and 19v.

holding.notes: The Prose Life is cataloged as item 1 at ff. 1–49 and closes with the explicit transcribed in both the catalog and Westlake.

physical_description.extent: A three-quire booklet occupying ff. 1r–49r within the complete codex
notes: At least one leaf is missing between ff. 18v and 19r, and at least one between ff. 19v and 20r; catchwords and narrative discontinuity establish the losses.
The catalog states that the manuscript was written by and for Thornton of East Newton, North Yorkshire. That association does not prove the anonymous translation was composed there.
The witness is unique, but the anonymous work is not identical to the physical codex or to Thornton's scribal act.

entities.label: Lincoln Cathedral catalog photograph of part of MS 91, f. 80r
entities.description: The one public image attached to the current whole-codex catalog record. It shows part of f. 80r and has no overlap with the PLAlex component at ff. 1r–49r.
notes: This partial contextual facsimile is not presented as work-image coverage.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Westlake's 1913 EETS edition
entities.description: A complete 132-page scan package for the Toronto copy of Westlake's edition, including PDF, OCR, metadata, and scandata.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete digital scan with OCR and scan metadata

entities.label: The Thornton Manuscript (Lincoln Cathedral MS 91), Scolar Press facsimile
entities.description: The complete printed photographic facsimile of Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 used throughout Chappell's edition, with introductions by D. S. Brewer and A. E. B. Owen.

bibliographic.citation: The Thornton Manuscript (Lincoln Cathedral MS 91), introductions by D. S. Brewer and A. E. B. Owen (London: Scolar Press, 1975; revised edition 1977).
bibliographic.title: The Thornton Manuscript (Lincoln Cathedral MS 91)

entities.label: Julie A. Chappell's The Prose Alexander of Robert Thornton
entities.description: A 1989 dissertation containing a critical study and a new interleaved Middle English and Modern English edition of the unique witness, followed by textual notes.

bibliographic.citation: Julie A. Chappell, The Prose Alexander of Robert Thornton: A Critical Edition (PhD diss., University of Washington, 1989).
bibliographic.title: The Prose Alexander of Robert Thornton: A Critical Edition
bibliographic.pages: 308; edition at pp. 49–243 and textual notes at pp. 244–271

notes: Chappell states that she relied throughout on the Scolar facsimile and on Míċeál Vaughan's notes from the original manuscript for physical and color features.
Vaughan's notes were not retrieved as a separately identifiable source, so this record does not assert that Chappell directly consulted the Lincoln codex.
Her edition and study are independent modern scholarly layers, not sources of the 1997 U-M encoding.

entities.label: Historia de preliis Alexandri Magni, J3 recension
entities.description: The variable Latin J3 textual tradition translated and adapted by the anonymous Middle English Prose Life of Alexander. This is a work-level source description, not a claim for any particular surviving manuscript or early printed exemplar.
notes: The physical Latin exemplar behind the Middle English version remains unidentified.

entities.label: Historia Alexandri Magni de preliis, Strasbourg, 17 March 1489
entities.description: A post-medieval Latin J3 print explicitly named with the 1494 Strasbourg print in one Westlake variant note. The lineage schema has no early-print type, so scholarly_edition is used as the closest available bibliographic entity type without implying modern editorial method.

bibliographic.citation: Historia Alexandri Magni de preliis (Strasbourg: Printer of Jordanus of Quedlinburg, 17 March 1489), GW 877; ISTC ia00398000.
bibliographic.title: Historia Alexandri Magni de preliis

notes: This print postdates the c. 1430–1450 Middle English witness and cannot be its physical source.
The cached Marburg copy is a representative extant exemplar of the edition, not a copy proved to have been used by Westlake.

entities.label: Marburg University Library facsimile of the 1489 Strasbourg Historia de preliis
entities.description: A complete 42-page scan of Marburg University Library shelfmark XVI A 49 p # Incun., with a METS description.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete PDF facsimile with METS metadata

entities.label: Historia Alexandri Magni de preliis, Strasbourg, 26 May 1494
entities.description: A post-medieval Latin J3 print explicitly named with the 1489 Strasbourg print in one Westlake variant note. The lineage schema has no early-print type, so scholarly_edition is used as the closest available bibliographic entity type without implying modern editorial method.

bibliographic.citation: Historia Alexandri Magni de preliis (Strasbourg: Printer of Jordanus of Quedlinburg, 26 May 1494), GW 879; ISTC ia00400000.
bibliographic.title: Historia Alexandri Magni de preliis

notes: This print postdates the c. 1430–1450 Middle English witness and cannot be its physical source.
The cached Heidelberg copy is a representative extant exemplar of the edition, not a copy proved to have been used by Westlake.

entities.label: Heidelberg University Library IIIF facsimile of the 1494 Strasbourg Historia de preliis
entities.description: The complete published IIIF sequence for Heidelberg University Library shelfmark I 3672 qt. ink: 74 canvases labeled 1–73 with an additional 19b.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest and 74 JPEG page images

rights.component: Pinned XML artifact and its encoded editorial text
rights.basis: The pinned XML embeds a legacy availability notice allowing personal, research, and teaching uses while prohibiting redistribution by commercial and non-profit content providers. The repository root's Unlicense is worded for software, and its application to the XML/data is not explicit.
notes: This records the exact artifact's embedded terms without treating them as copyright ownership or as identical to current U-M discovery metadata.

rights.component: Current U-M digital item and encoded text
rights.basis: The current U-M item discovery record labels the materials public domain and No Copyright–United States.
notes: The provider assertion is recorded separately from the narrower historical notice embedded in the pinned XML.

rights.component: Current Compendium XML serialization and encoded text
rights.basis: The current XML's availability statement says the work has been identified as public domain, free of known copyright restrictions, and may be copied, modified, distributed, and performed without permission, including commercially.
notes: This current provider assertion is kept separate from the legacy restriction embedded in the pinned artifact.

rights.component: 1913 edition text and Westlake's editorial matter
rights.basis: The work was published in 1913 and the U-M/Internet Archive records treat the source as public domain or not in copyright in the United States.

The cached Internet Archive metadata records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the United States. It does not supply an explicit license, warranty, or provider-contract statement for the scan derivatives.
notes: The underlying 1913 edition's public-domain status is modeled separately.

rights.basis: The medieval text and manuscript copy date to c. 1430–1450.
notes: This does not determine rights or contractual conditions in modern photographs or newly supplied reproductions.

rights.component: Lincoln Cathedral catalog photograph of part of f. 80r
rights.basis: The catalog record identifies Lincoln Cathedral Library copyright and states that permission of the Dean and Chapter is required.
notes: The image does not overlap PLAlex, but its provider terms are not generalized into rights for all manuscript reproductions.

rights.component: Underlying medieval Latin J3 source work
rights.basis: The medieval Latin source-work tradition predates modern copyright terms by centuries.
notes: This does not determine rights in modern editions, transcriptions, database records, or facsimiles.

rights.component: Future digital images or photocopies supplied by Lincoln Cathedral Library
rights.basis: Images are requestable for a fee, and the site's terms reserve rights and distinguish personal reference use from licensed commercial use; no PLAlex reproduction order was placed.
The modern printed facsimile and scholarly introductions were published in 1975 and revised in 1977; the consulted Wellcome catalog does not provide a reusable digital license.

The dissertation copyright page states Copyright 1989 Julie Chappell, all rights reserved, permits library inspection, and limits extensive copying to scholarly fair use while directing reproduction through UMI.
The Latin print was published in 1489.

rights.component: Marburg digital facsimile and METS record
rights.basis: The Marburg METS record applies the Creative Commons Public Domain Mark 1.0 to the digitized object.
The Latin print was published in 1494.

rights.component: Heidelberg IIIF manifest and page images
rights.basis: The published Heidelberg IIIF manifest identifies the Creative Commons Public Domain Mark 1.0.

editorial_practices.representation: Expanded in the printed Middle English transcription
editorial_practices.description: Westlake generally expands manuscript abbreviations rather than reproducing them diplomatically.
editorial_practices.scope: The surviving Middle English transcription at printed p. 8 through p. 115

editorial_practices.representation: A manuscript-collated reading text, with some emendation and inconsistent apparatus
editorial_practices.description: E. M. Thompson collated Westlake's text against the manuscript; Chappell judges the transcription generally accurate while documenting emendation and inconsistent recording of departures.

editorial_practices.source_feature: An acephalous opening and manuscript losses after ff. 18v and 19v
editorial_practices.representation: Modern English translations from an unidentified Latin text on printed pp. 1–8, 46–48, and 50–52
editorial_practices.description: Westlake supplies missing narrative in modern translation rather than leaving three gaps or silently presenting the supplies as Middle English.
editorial_practices.scope: The opening and two internal supply zones

editorial_practices.source_feature: Westlake's complete publication, including prefatory note, reading text, marginal references, footnotes, and other apparatus
editorial_practices.representation: The reading text and six notes are encoded; the prefatory note and most apparatus are omitted
editorial_practices.description: The U-M encoding is a selective representation of the edition even though it includes the entire reading text.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source, PLAlex.xml at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Establishes the exact repository artifact, U-M identifiers and production credits, Westlake source declaration, embedded legacy availability notice, language-header error, Modern English ADD supplies, Middle English body, Latin passages, and explicit.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of the pinned PLAlex.xml.
evidence.locator: BODY; DIV1, P, PB, MILESTONE, ADD, NOTE1, Q1, L, and FOREIGN elements
evidence.summary: Counts 8 DIV1, 178 P, 114 PB, 98 manuscript MILESTONE, 11 ADD, 6 NOTE1, 4 verse Q1, and 8 L elements. The L elements form one prophecy cluster at printed pp. 94–95 / manuscript f. 40r: three Latin couplets and one Middle English couplet.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME LICENSE at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Contains an Unlicense dedication worded for software. Its applicability to source XML and data is not explicit and does not erase the narrower notice embedded in PLAlex.xml.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English item PLAlex.
evidence.summary: Identifies the exact public digital text and current public-domain / No Copyright–United States discovery statement.
notes: The item was verified through current indexed U-M discovery results; direct automated retrieval met Cloudflare anti-bot handling.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium, current PLAlex XML, last modified 9 May 2026.
evidence.summary: Provides the complete current ETS serialization, public-domain availability statement, Westlake source description, editorial declaration, and full reading text.
notes: HTTP Last-Modified was Sat, 09 May 2026 15:36:56 GMT; the provider exposes no named release identifier.

evidence.citation: Target-scoped structural and text-stream comparison of pinned PLAlex.xml with the cached current provider PLAlex.xml.
evidence.summary: After Unicode NFKC normalization, removal of non-alphanumeric characters, and exclusion of the current file's two CORR alternatives in favor of its SIC readings, both BODY text streams contain 190,490 characters and have SHA-256 94a0ed0f045b2725f4750acc8e0679821949712e25cdf8ee9529b5b506b3b37b. Both preserve 173 BODY P, 114 PB, 98 MILESTONE, 11 ADD, 11 HEAD, and 8 L elements, while wrapper and element vocabulary differ.
notes: The comparison excludes markup and does not claim the XML files are byte-identical.
The current file uses ETS, Q, HI, NOTE, CHOICE, CORR, and SIC structures where the pinned file uses DLPSTEXTCLASS-era vocabulary.

evidence.citation: The Prose Life of Alexander from the Thornton MS., ed. J. S. Westlake, EETS o.s. 143 (London, 1913, for 1911).

evidence.summary: Establishes the edition's title, date, credits, manuscript basis, Modern English lacuna supplies, exact printed-page boundaries, explicit, and the selected p. 6 note naming the 1489 and 1494 editions.

open_questions.question: Which physical Latin exemplar or exemplars underlie the anonymous Middle English translation and Westlake's three Modern English supplies?
next_steps: Consult specialized source studies and textual collations across J3 manuscripts and early prints.
Do not infer an exemplar from broad recension agreement or Westlake's isolated citation of the 1489 and 1494 prints.
notes: The J3 recension relationship is supported, but its textual variation prevents a physical-exemplar claim from the consulted evidence.

notes: The negative result concerns public access only; the institution may hold unpublished images.

open_questions.question: Can the anonymous Middle English translation be dated or localized more narrowly than its attestation by the c. 1430–1450 East Newton-associated witness?
next_steps: Review dialectal and linguistic studies of the Prose Alexander independently from codex production evidence.
Do not substitute Thornton's scribal location for the work's place of composition.
